content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What horror movie made you sit in silence after the credits?

**Score:** 177
**Body:** Not looking for “fun scary.” I want the kind of horror movie that leaves you staring at the ceiling at 2am questioning why you watched it in the first place.

Could be psychological, found footage, slow burn, disturbing, or just deeply uncomfortable. I’m trying to find movies that feel cursed in the best way possible.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Opening night of Blair Witch Project, when everyone still thought it was real, the entire theater stayed through the credits. When the “all characters are fictitious” disclaimer appeared, everyone booed and left. 😂

Still the best marketing I’ve seen to this day. 

Edit: I am by no means recommending this movie! Just the only time that’s happened to me.
  - When I saw that it was on two burned cd’s before a trailer had even come out and no one knew what it was. Someone had just given me them and told me to watch. It was basically the best marketing there could have been for that movie. So fucking scary. Just BWP 1 & 2 scribbled on the cds. About a month later a website appears and a trailer followed. That haunted me like crazy. 
2. *Martyrs* \- I think that "questioning why you watched it in the first place" was the whole point.
3. The first SAW. 
4. Se7en. I still hate when I get a box delivered to my place because of that damn movie.
  - “What’s in the box?” 👩
5. Silence of the Lambs. 

In high school. In the theatre. 
6. **Ju On: The Grudge (2002)**  
It personally hits every one of my fears and I slept with a light on afterwards.

**The Mist (2007)**  
Great build up of tension throughout.

**The Orphanage (2007)**

They're all better going in without much knowledge of them, IMO.
7. As Above So Below

Note: I used to do a lot of "urban exploration", and am a huge history buff; so, this movie resonated with me on a visceral level.
8. Midsommar 

## 2. Im looking for fun whacky obscure 80-90s movies.

**Score:** 62
**Body:** This started with me watching matilda last night. Im a big danny devito fan and god damn that film is a classic. Decided to watch some more Danny devito directed films. Ive watched, 

Matilda

Duplex

Throw mama from a train

The war of roses 

All danny devito films. He's a very talented director imo but I want more whacky 90s nostalgia things in vein of 

What about Bob

Planes trains and automobiles

Rat race

Bio-dome 

Harry and the Henderson's

Little shop of horrors 

Kingpin

The pest

Im naming mostly more well known. That's all I can think of and its bothering me because I have actually seen a lot of them throughout my life lol but I want obscure. Obvthey dont even have to be good 😂 because it still fulfills my deep yearning to return to that simpler time 🫠so really the quality is irrelevant. If theyre "good" films with good actors thats just a bonus. Lol

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Its not obscure, but Twins isn't on your list.

Also, Ruthless people is one of my favourite movies
  -  Ruthless People , yes!
2. Better Off Dead, compared to a lot of movies that came out at the same time, is pretty obscure I’d say. It’s *almost* a parody film. It’s great, especially if you’re going through a breakup. 
  - You owe me two dollars!
3. PEE-WEE’S BIG ADVENTURE!!! It is so wacky and chaotic but at the same time creepy, it’s a must watch Tim Burton film 
4. For a little more Danny Devito, try *Johnny Dangerously*
5. Joe Versus the Volcano 


6. These are all movies I grew up on!
For the 2nd time today I recommend Serial Mom, directed by John Waters, fun whacky and obscure.

7. Very Bad Things. Hoo boy it's a ride. 
8. Have you seen Bowfinger?

## 3. Looking for some swampy bayou horror, mystery, or thrillers.

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Recently watched The Skeleton Key and while it left a lot to be desired, I really liked the setting. Caddo Lake was pretty good too but I want something GRITTY.

Edit: Damn ya’ll came THROUGH!! I’m stoked to work my way through this list. I love Reddit ❣️

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's not a movie but *True Detective* Season 1 is pretty amazing TV that is right up your alley.
2. Mud, 2012 with Matthew McConaughey is a swampy thriller and very gritty.
3. Not a movie, but the first season of True Detective would fit here. It's a *fantastic* show,  very dark & gritty. That season is worth the investment
4. Southern Comfort (1981)


Members of the Louisiana guard embark on a training mission into the bayou. Things turn deadly when they anger a Cajun hunting party.


Stars Powers Boothe, Fred Ward, Brion James and Keith Carradine.


Good film.


Edit: forgot to memtion it's directed by Walter Hill, who also did 'The Warriors'
5. The Gift (2000, the one with Cate Blanchett)
6. Angel Heart
7. Down By Law

The Peanut Butter Falcon

Deliverance
8. Gator
9. Tucker & Dale vs. Evil (2011)
10. Midnight in the Garden of Good and Evil

## 4. Good Movie/ Bad Adaptation

**Score:** 20
**Body:** What are some suggestions for movies that are great on their own, but end up missing the point of their source material? An example for me would be the 2005 Pride and Prejudice adaptation. It works quite well as a romantic drama, but it misses most of the satire of the original novel. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Annihilation 
  - Seriously. It's almost an entirely different thing than the book, but it's still a good movie. I honestly think I like movie adaptions better this way. They took a good idea and made something else good with it.
2. The Shining is probably the most famous example of this. King hates it.
3. Blade Runner
4. The Shining

Starship Troopers

Maybe not missed the point, more changed the focus.

Almost completed ignored the book (other than characters and title) but still a great film:

The Sum of All Fears
  - I Am Legend 
5. Emerald Fennell's recent *"Wuthering Heights",* as evinced by the quotation marks, is intentionally not an adaptation of *Wuthering Heights*, but a delightfully horny and gorgeous riff on the first half of it.
6. Stanley Kubrick's The Shining. 

It's a great movie on its own, but it changes the source material in very big ways.
7. They completely changed the endings of I believe all three Dan Brown movies. 
8. Frankenstein (1931)

## 5. Movies that helped you reconnect with watching films after an emotional burnout?

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I used to watch a lot of movies with someone I was very close to. Watching films, sharing recommendations, and discussing them afterward was a huge part of our friendship.

After we drifted apart about a year ago, I slowly stopped enjoying movies altogether because almost every film reminded me of that time. I went from watching around 20 movies a month to barely finishing even one movie this past year.

I’m trying to reconnect with movies again, but I’m looking for films that feel emotionally “safe” and easy to get absorbed into without triggering nostalgia too much.

Not really looking for romance-heavy films. Maybe uplifting, comforting, funny, adventurous, visually immersive, or just genuinely engaging movies that helped you during a rough phase in life.

Open to any genre except heavy romance

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Marcel the Shell with shoes on. It will realign you mentally and make you rediscover what's actually important
2. The fall(2006)  
re released two years ago in my country. Went to theatre on a whim that day and it magically helped me reconnect to movies after a very rough, burnt out, no-movie-at-all few years of my life. It even fits your genre criteria(although it's not emotionally light...). It's not for everyone(but after all.. no movie is really for everyone. Is it?) highly highly recommend.
3. The 100 Year-Old Man Who Climbed Out the Window and Disappeared (2013)

A Man Called Ove (2015)

Hundreds of Beavers (2022)

Corpus Christi (2019)

Boy (2010

Frances Ha (2012)

Scrapper (2023)
4. I loved life of Pi. 

lion

Slumdog millionaire
5. Perks of Being a Wallflower


6. Captain Fantastic. It’s not for everyone, but I love it and it gives me hope. 
7. The Truman Show

Rental Family
8. A Thousand Clowns restored my faith in life
9. Paper Moon (1973)
10. What got me back into movies after a multiple year's long stint with watching very few was watching competencey porn movies, starting with Tetris and BlackBerry. Then Sorkin's Steve Jobs movie, Joy, Air, Moneyball, and The Big Short.

Once I remembered how much I love movies and how much I missed a contained story within 1.5-3hrs I was back on the train.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What, to you, is the saddest real-world death of an actor that ended up impacting a franchise?

**Score:** 4833
**Body:** I can think of several, but to me, the loss of Gloria Foster as the Oracle in The Matrix was so jarring that I basically refused to accept that she had been replaced in the final movie. It's nothing against Mary Alice, but Gloria was so uniquely suited to the role and she had such a beautiful and compelling energy about her, in my mind she is simply irreplaceable. I would rather have seen her character written out, or rather "deprogrammed" in some way, than have the actress replaced. In fact, I think the jarring effect of her character "looking different" due to some (I don't remember exactly) problem with the Matrix itself was one of the key factors in why the final part of the trilogy just didn't work for me. It was weirdly handled. 

So what's your most upsetting loss of an actor in a movie franchise? 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Judith Barsi was the original voice of Ducky in the 1988 animated film *The Land Before Time*. Known for her signature phrase "Yep! Yep! Yep!", Barsi was a talented child actress who also voiced Anne-Marie in *All Dogs Go to Heaven*. She tragically died at age 10 in 1988 before the film's release. She was killed in a murder suicide by her father 

![gif](giphy|ZNRARhcjUuSuQ)
2. Leonard Nimoy and Anton Yelchin for the Star Trek reboot series. Nimoy got to have a big, full career but Yelchin was so young. the reboots didnt continue for other reasons but idk if the ensemble would have worked as well without them.
  - Anton Yelchin's death is still so sad as it was such a freak accident and he definitely had a talented career ahead of him. I think of all these blockbusters being made by top tier caliber directors, and how many of them he could have found a place in. He seemed well respected, loved etc. Definitely a sad one even a decade later.

EDIT - also was a huge trollhunters fan so it fits the question for how much of an impact it had to me for that series. My wife and I used to call that kids Game of Thrones as it was so epic and addictive (and we don't even have kids lol - just appreciate good animation, a good story and all things Guillermo del Toro)
  - I strongly agree with this. As a matter of fact, Anton Yelchin was one of the people I thought of when I wrote this post.
    - His death impacted the Odd Thomas franchise too I believe. A 7 book series only got one movie. I still watch that one every couple months. 
3. For me, Jim Henson.  The Jim Henson company is a shadow of what it was when he was still alive. 
4. Andy in Spartacus

![gif](giphy|10sxMbo7MLw9he)
5. ![gif](giphy|1is1enAFIG6Wc)


6. ![gif](giphy|QtY8XLTfUcHwk)
  - This kid had such promise. Absolutely tragic. 

## 2. Favorite absolutely unhinged character? [ Begbie from Trainspotting (1996)~ played by Robert Carlyle ]

**Score:** 475

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dennis Hopper in Blue Velvet
  - ![gif](giphy|mYZnB29KHRrxe)
2. Daniel Day Lewis as Bill the butcher in Gangs of New York
3. ![gif](giphy|VtYzoUfAcCSXe)

  
Cable Guy
4. Brad Pitt- 12 Monkeys 
5. Benicio in Fear and Loathing 
  - Benicio in Sin City 
6. Gary Oldman.  In just about every role he's played.
7. ‘Brick Top’ (Alan Ford) in Snatch…
“Sugar?”, “No thanks, Turkish…I’m sweet enough!”
8. Ben Kingsley as Don Logan in *Sexy Beast.* "Yes, Grosvenor! *Yes*, Rowntree!" Bonkers performance. 

## 3. What’s your favorite interrogation scene in movies? My pick would be the Joker interrogation scene in The Dark Knight.

**Score:** 376

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/kcci5m9emwzg1.jpeg?width=1024&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=e13b8fc5719791e41eff972ce9740c489a35e2f9

Silence of the Lambs
2. Oh this is an easy one.  They interrogate two prisoners in two different rooms in LA Confidential.  But what the audience is really learning is the character and talents of the two leads, Guy Pierce (cunning) and Russell Crowe (brutal) who both illicit quick confessions in vastly different ways.  The entire point of the movie is that unfortunately both types are needed for “justice” and it is shown best in this scene. Come to think of it, I that is also one of the lessons in Batman.
  - Ones brutally cunning and another is cunningly brutal

Got it

WAAAAAAAGHHH
3. I loved the advice Joker gave Batman:     

You never start with the head it makes the brain all fuzzy and decreases the effectiveness of the next action           
   
Batman punches Jokers hand that is on the table      

Joker still groggy See what I mean?
4. Opening of inglorious basterds. I used to teach interrogation and I’d show that as an example- also bc I just wanted to show it in class. 
5. It’s cheating because it’s TV - but Rust Cohle’s interview of the woman who confesses to killing her child, Season 1 of True Detective.




The way he absolutely breaks her down into confession and then flips on a dime and is completely devoid of empathy when she does admit it. 





Brilliantly written, fantastically performed by all involved. 
6. Is this your homework, Larry?
7. *True Romance* was full of memorable scenes, when the mob was looking for Clarence. There was one with Brad Pitt, another with Dennis Hopper, and another with Patricia Arquette…
8. https://i.redd.it/p9c31lidtwzg1.gif
9. "The Interview" with Hugo Weaving. 

## 4. We heard your male unhinged characters, now let’s hear your favorite female unhinged characters [Pearl played by Mia Goth in Pearl 2022]

**Score:** 333

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|YPflpFTWLWXF6)

Debbie Jellinsky ❤️
  - https://preview.redd.it/enza1wvkwzzg1.jpeg?width=1206&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=dfa4cfe7389680471461c0c813c0769ba6481a91

🙌🖤
2. Fairuza Balk in The Craft 
  - Fairuza Balk in pretty much anything, yeah?

![gif](giphy|11CNLlmNAQXIli)
3. Wednesday addams.
  - “Yikes”  
![gif](giphy|vMOLPykb4lXws)
4. Annie Wilkes  


https://preview.redd.it/nvg17uv3tzzg1.jpeg?width=853&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=3e4184c914d6ff1714624c56556ef86a9ac4e205
5. ![gif](giphy|FFxaEQafd60YU)


6. Amy Dunne in Gone Girl   
![gif](giphy|X7RrVqY2REtUI)
7. ![gif](giphy|10Jg7krSvcCQes)

Azula - ATLA

## 5. What your favorite film where an actor that is known for playing bad guys plays a good guy? Mine is Danny Trejo as the wise supportive if out there biker from Bubble Boy. He was having so much fun poking fun at himself.

**Score:** 291

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Danny Trejo in Spy Kids too
  - I came here to mention this. 
2. Danny Trejo is a really nice guy too. Everthing I hear about him is that hes just a cool dude. My wife met him once and said he was really short, really funny, and really nice. (her words)
3. Alan Rickman in Dogma
  - The story Kevin Smith tells of Alan Rickman and Jason Mewes having an intense conversation with each other always makes me smile
4. Christopher Walken - Joe Dirt and Wedding Crashers 
5. I’ve seen Alan Rickman listed three times already, for three different movies. 

That man was a gem. 
6. Alan Rickman in Galaxy Quest.
7. Alan Rickman as Marvin in HHGttG
  - Harry Hotter Gobbin the tasty gabagool?

Just kidding, hitchhikers guide will always be fantastic, and if you've never read the book I highly recommend!

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. George Lucas on the differences between Soviet and US cinema, “Russian filmmakers had more freedom than I had”

**Score:** 280
**Body:** “One of the reasons I retired is so I can make movies that aren’t popular, because in the world we live in - and the system we've created for ourselves, in terms of it's a big industry - you cannot lose money. So the point is that you're forced to make a particular kind of movie. And I used to say this all the time, with people, you know, back when Russia was the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics, and they'd say: "Oh, but aren’t you so glad that you're in America?" And I'd say: "Well, I know a lot of Russian filmmakers and they have a lot more freedom than I have. All they have to do is be careful about criticizing the government. Otherwise, they can do anything they want" - George Lucas 2015. 

George Lucas was mostly referring to the late 1960s and early 1970s, specifically when he was making THX 1138 with the help of Francis Ford Coppola within the traditional US studio system. He described the situation as needing to “trick” the studio to get them on board with his vison, but it still didn’t really work out. Ironically, this is how many Soviet artists described their experiences when trying to get projects made within the Soviet state system, from novels to albums to films. A filmmaker like Oliver Stone would never exist in the Soviet Union. 

This poor experience led to George Lucas essentially “controlling the means of production” on his later films. He had his own companies, aka full control over the filmmaking. 

Do you think George Lucas has a point about US cinema? 

The Soviet state was cutting up films like Andrei Rublev (1966) because it may have been indirectly criticizing government policy (Andrei Tarkovsky never considered himself a dissident), but US studios were cutting up films like THX 1138 because they found it too uncommercial or experimental for “mainstream audiences.” You can also say that about a film like Easy Rider (1969), but that still made millions and was critically and culturally acclaimed.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. As someone who’s immersed themselves in Soviet history, he’s more or less correct.

There was a lot of censorship in the USSR, but even then, if the satire was subtle or subtextual enough, the government would either not notice or let it slide. As Lucas said, you had to be careful, but some degree of, say, satire or allegory was allowed (like in the case of Kin-dza-dza).

Some older Russian film professors have told me that, creatively, there was a fair degree of freedom and experimentation that you weren’t afforded in capitalist countries.
  - This is accurate, specifically to periods outside 30s-53. 

You can make the (Western centric) argument the world became more liberal, more counter-culture, in the late 1960s and early 1970s, which the Soviet Union “fell behind” but the fact there was less monetary incentive in the Soviet Union, definitely created less pressure for filmmakers. 

It’s not like you can depict sex in 70s Soviet cinema, but once you were able to, no one will be asking “how much less box office will we lose for this” 

Warner Bros literally picked a McDonalds deal over making Batman some violent film franchise (literally just PG-13) in the 1990s. The fact toy deals are picked before the film script shows how these studios are ran. “But can we make Mona Lisa toys?”
    - > Warner Bros literally picked a McDonalds deal over making Batman some violent film franchise (literally just PG-13) in the 1990s. 

Isn't this the only screen version of Batman that uses guns and kills bad guys?
      - The funny part is that Returns wasn’t even that violent itself (what led to the change), it’s just the power of McDonalds that led to the studio to change up the franchise with Forever. 

“Merchandising, where the real money from the movie is made” 
2. He was right to a certain extent.


You would still have to get your films approved and it wasn't uncommon to have them be censored if there were any scenes that were considered subversive, but Russia specifically valued cultural exchange.


My spouse is Russian and she mentioned Tarkovsky not being well liked by the government at the time.  I was a bit confused so I asked her why they let him keep making films and funding them.  She acted like I was insane and said, "Are you serious?  Everyone else loved him and he won awards.  They weren't going to give that up."


It's easy to say American 70's New Wave films offered filmmakers way more freedom, which I'd say is probably true, but those were the result of small studios pumping out easily marketed genre pictures first to get the money required to make the art house stuff.  Something like Easy Rider wouldn't have existed without Bob Rafelson pumping out episodes of The Monkees for several years first.
  - And it’s important “cultural exchange” doesn’t get interpreted into “propaganda soft power” because this is how it’s been in Europe for centuries. Soviet Union was hardly unique in this regard, because every state uses their Michelangelo, Shakespeare, Tarkovsky, Mbappe (even sports) to legitimatize and promote themselves. 

It’s ironic because Akira Kurosawa (loved in the West) wasn’t even that liked in his native Japan. He went to foreign countries, including the Soviet Union, to continue his career after it started to stall in Japan. 

Artists have always been quite internationalist and global. Tarkovsky’s “defection” is Cold War centric framing. I once thought the Soviet Union was basically “sabotaging” his career, like many other artists, when they were actually supporting it for nearly two decades. 
3. Lucas has been claiming he is going to go work on small little art movies since the '70s, and he has never done it.  I think he is the kind of guy that likes to think of himself as a maverick challenging the oppressive Hollywood system, but the reality is that most of his life has been the perfect outcome of that Hollywood system.
4. I guess in the US, you can make whatever kind of movie you want, as long as you pay for it.

I'm sure Soviet filmmakers also had producers who were keen to get an ROI. 

And the most cynical point: George Lucas' *worst* movies were made when he had unlimited free reign. 
  - > George Lucas' worst movies were made when he had unlimited free reign.

Yeah, that is highly ironic when you think about it. Seeing the first 90s Star Wars film in the theaters was my first experience in being disappointed by a legendary director. He just couldn't see the forest for the trees, hyper focused on tech over story and quality of acting. 
    - To me another important detail to remember is that by the time George did the prequels he'd been out of the game for years. He famously had a feud with the Director's Guild and was notorious for being a Hollywood outsider; his special effects company kept him as a significant presence but otherwise he'd removed himself from the core of the industry.

## 2. All Quiet on the Western Front (1930) — why nothing made since has surpassed it

**Score:** 28
**Body:** There's a structural choice Lewis Milestone makes 

in All Quiet on the Western Front that I keep 

thinking about.

The film opens in a classroom. A teacher delivers 

a passionate speech about glory, honour and duty. 

The boys enlist that same day, intoxicated by his 

words.

Near the end of the film Paul returns home on leave. 

He visits that same classroom. The same teacher is 

delivering the same speech to a new group of boys.

Nothing has changed. Nothing has been learned.

The machine keeps feeding.

That single structural decision — the classroom 

bookend — says everything the film needs to say 

about war, propaganda and institutional failure. 

No modern war film has made that point more 

economically or more devastatingly.

The Nazis understood what this film was doing. 

They organised riots to shut down screenings. 

Multiple governments banned or censored it.

Because it didn't show war as tragedy.

It showed war as a system designed to consume 

young men while the people who designed it 

remained untouched.

The 2022 remake is technically accomplished but 

it never lands this punch. It shows the horror 

of war. The 1930 version shows the machinery 

of war. That's a completely different and more 

devastating argument.

I've been thinking about why the classroom scene 

works so much better than anything in the remake 

and I think it's because Milestone trusts the 

audience completely. He doesn't explain the irony. 

He just shows it and walks away.

The full film is on YouTube if anyone wants to 

watch it — I uploaded it with chapters so you 

can navigate to specific scenes.

https://youtu.be/uUVR-bwTHHQ

Curious what others think — is there a war film 

made since 1930 that makes a more powerful 

structural argument against war than this one?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Check out the movie Gallipoli, by director Peter Weir! It's so often overlooked and has an incredible structure which puts you directly in the mindset of a young man and his friends preparing for a "great adventure" only to discover the adventure is the most harrowing experience you can ever have. 
  - Gallipoli is a great shout. What makes those films hit so hard is that they begin with this youthful idea of war as purpose or adventure, then slowly strip all of that away. The contrast between innocence and reality is probably what makes anti-war films linger with people for years afterwards.
2. Some contemporary films such as the French"Wooden Crosses" about soldiers anxiety about tunnelers  under their trenches and the German "Westfront" are equally powerful. Johnny Got His Gun is strong meat and not a film I could watch again.
  - Yeah, Wooden Crosses and Westfront really capture that same psychological pressure in a different way — especially the sense of waiting and paranoia rather than just open combat.

I think what makes those films hit so hard is exactly that focus on anticipation and fear of what you can’t see, not just the violence itself.

And I agree on Johnny Got His Gun, it’s powerful, but it’s almost overwhelming in a way that doesn’t invite repeat viewing. It’s less “war film” and more a kind of sustained psychological experience.
3. The only war movie I've seen that comes close to the same moral and emotional heft is Paths of Glory, which was also banned in France when it was released I believe.

I last watched All Quiet... decades ago and some of the scenes are still etched on my memory.
  - It is a tough watch. There's a few movies ive seen like this, left an impression years later. 
4. I'm going to have to see this. I've read the book but never seen the film. (I was actually expecting the book to have more emotional heft to it; it has such a reputation, and it didn't hit me like I expected.) 
  - I'll now have to read the book in return! The film hits hard, enjoy the butterfly ending! 🥲
    - From my memory (although it’s been awhile) the book has both of those classroom scenes as well. Although the internal monologue of the main character makes his feelings and thoughts on the situation more explicit. 

But I think you’ll like the book.

## 3. Swingers: Last night’s 35mm screening with director Doug Liman, producer and music supervisor

**Score:** 24
**Body:** What an incredible evening.  We saw a nice 35mm print of the film and then a discussion with the team behind the film.  I took a younger associate that works on my film projects, who never saw the film and he absolutely loved it.  

The team relayed how no one wanted to finance the film and the two years of doing public table reads looking for investors, with no luck.  

After eventually getting $275k in private investment, they made the film and had to show it to Spielberg to get approval for the Jaws theme music, since Steven wasn’t giving approvals due to so many parodying the film.  Now only was Steven so impressed he gave the approval, but then hired Vince Vaughn for Jurassic Park.  

The camera was noisy and Liman had his jacket and comforter wrapped around the camera for the dialog scenes.  

We were able to speak with the director and producer afterwards, which was nice as many of these events you can’t speak with them afterwards.  

https://imgur.com/gallery/swingers-screening-discussion-with-director-VVWm7RP

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Favreau's gamble was an "all or nothing" one: he turned down huge sums (for an unemployed actor at the time) just to avoid seeing his friends replaced with A-list stars. If he had accepted Johnny Depp to play the role of Mike, we would have had a commercially "correct" film, but we would have missed out on seeing their chemistry. An entire generation.
2. There's a fascinating irony in the fact that a team that spent two years getting rejected at every public reading ended up creating the film that defined the concept of 'so money' for an entire generation. Seeing Swingers on 35mm isn't just nostalgia, it's a lesson in texture; the film's grain perfectly reflects the low-budget energy that forced the team to bet everything on dialogue and charisma, not effects.
  - There actually was some interest in the script, if it could be completely rewritten and starting known actors (none of the cast) Faverou explored that route for a time, before trying to get it made himself.  

Once the film was shot and had a rough cut, it became the talk of the town with several companies vieng for distribution.  The team wanted Miramax since they could get it a wide release.  
  - So clearly an A.I. comment
    - A.I. would know how to spell “vying.”
    - Why do you think that? I liked the movie. According to you, everything is AI.

## 4. Superbad will go down as one of my favorite comedy films. But I feel that almost all the good parts happen before the adult party. Not saying that the latter half is bad (Michael Cera singing is cinema gold), but I feel that there is a difference in quality. What are other great films like this?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** Again, the movie is not bad, even the latter half of the film is still a 7-7.5/10 for me. Is this just a case of the first half of the film filled with so many great scenes and one-liners?

As someone who is not American. The only reason why I know Scottsdale Arizona is from that cocaine guy. It's probably one of the few things I know about Arizona in general apart from the Tea, Navajo code talkers, and Steve Nash.

## 5. I have just watched The Only Son (1936), my first Ozu film

**Score:** 5
**Body:** I have been meaning to get into the work of Yasujiro Ozu for quite some time now, I have frequently seen *Tokyo Story* (1953) at the top of many "best films of all time" lists, and  I wanted to watch more Japanese films that were made by directors other than Akira Kurosawa.  
I wanted to watch one of Ozu's previous films before watching the film many consider his "best" so I could get an understanding of his style so I could appreciate his other films more. I was initially going to watch *Late Spring* (1949), but the blu-ray I ordered of it came with *The Only Son* (1936) which I would go on to find out is Ozu's first sound film, so I decided to watch it first, and I must say, I am really impressed.

*The Only Son* (1936) is a film about a mother from a rural Japanese province who decided to do whatever she could to get her son, Ryosuke into secondary education, and visits him years later when he moved, and finds him as a teacher living in poverty.  
The film is a unique and successful attempt at capturing the difficulty of lower class life in and the financial hardship of it. At the beginning of the film, the mother is dismissive of higher education, because she is a widow and had insufficient money to send young Ryosuke to secondary education, but went through with it, in the hopes of helping her son have a better life. When the mother visits Ryosuke years later, he does what he can to make sure his mother has the best experience she can in Tokyo, and pulls as many strings as he can to financially afford it. The people around Ryosuke and his family are also struggling in their own ways, as one of Ryosuke's neighbours can barely afford to buy toys for their children.

My favourite part of the film is the underlying theme of anti-fatalism in the face of hopelessness and hardship. Ryosuke's mother put herself through financial hardship so her son could live better than what he was born with, later in the film after the time jump it is revealed her financial situation is even worse than at the beginning, but she refuses to give up in order to be there for her son. Ryosuke gave up hope in trying to move up, demoralised by his social standing as a teacher, and that his teacher who motivated him to purse education ended up in poverty as well cooking porkchops for a living. Despite this, Ryosuke does everything he can to do his best for his mother, and helps his neighbours when they have a medical crisis. Ryosuke throughout the film is very self-depricating believing he will never be more, but at the end of the film when shown of his potential and how life is not over just yet, he decides to not give up and study even further when his mother helped him regain hope.

In terms of filmmaking, Ozu's minimalistic style and approach to cinematography helped emphasise the emotion of the characters and I believe successfully made the film, and it's themes connect more with the audience. (Pardon if I do not have as much to say about the cinematography of this film as in my previous post here, this film struck me as one that puts more emphasis on the story and themes than cinematography (it is not bad at all, but the main draw I believe is in the story))  
I really enjoyed my first Ozu film and am looking forward to watching more of his work, what do you think of Ozu's first sound film?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I absolutely love Only Son.  Especially the cinematography!  This is the first classic Ozu, imo.  It is the first Ozu film with all the things that will become his hallmarks, and they are all masterfully executed.

* Classic Ozu cinematography. Excellent camera positioning, framing, depth of field, and lighting shot after shot, especially in domestic spaces, and brilliant use of observaional, slow-cinema long takes.  Thr cinematography exemplifies the Ozu orientation best expressed in the Japanese idea of ["mono no aware"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mono_no_aware?) -- empathy toward things, with an awareness of impermanence. Ozu has an unparalleled ability to hold human-made objects in contemplative and almost loving still shots.
* Yes, also the story.  Classic Ozu themes of intergenerational family melodrama and humanism, with emphasies on modernity and an empathy for people's inner sadness, especially those revolving around the fulfillment (or not) of familial roles and expectations. The epigraph at the beginning of the film is "Life’s tragedy begins with the bond between parent and child" -- I cannot imagine a better ten-word description of Ozu's approach to family dynamics.
